config.rationale: The best result so far is the clean vLLM baseline, so the next throughput-oriented experiment should stay on vLLM and increase effective batching headroom rather than return to SGLang speculative decoding, which already failed to become healthy once. I kept tp=1/pp=1/dp=1 because there is only one GPU. I also kept max_model_len at the already-proven-safe 65536 instead of raising context allocation, because for optimize_throughput a larger reserved KV budget would likely reduce admitted batch/concurrency on a single 80GB H100. The changes here are the highest-probability throughput levers on top of the current leader: raise gpu_memory_utilization from 0.9 to 0.95, enable chunked prefill, enable prefix caching, and add vLLM's --language-model-only because this checkpoint is a VLM and skipping vision components should free some VRAM. Expected gain versus the current vLLM baseline is roughly +5% to +15% throughput if the workload has enough concurrency, with minimal TTFT risk; most of that upside should come from extra memory headroom and better overlap of prefill/decode, while prefix caching helps if prompts share prefixes. For the user's SGLang MTP command at context-length 262144 (NEXTN, 3 steps, 4 draft tokens), my estimate is only about +5% to +15% net throughput versus non-speculative SGLang at the same 262144 context, and confidence is limited because the smaller-context speculative run already failed health checks; the huge KV reservation at 262144 is likely to eat part of the speculative gain, so zero gain or a regression is plausible on shorter or prefill-heavy mixes. For vLLM, the expected benefit of --language-model-only alone is usually modest but real on a VLM checkpoint: about +3% to +10% throughput at fixed context/concurrency, mostly by reclaiming memory from unused vision components; the upside is higher only if that saved VRAM is converted into materially more active KV cache or higher admitted batching. This makes the proposed vLLM configuration the best next benchmark because it directly exploits the current winning engine and attacks the throughput bottleneck with lower operational risk than another speculative SGLang attempt.

smoke_tests.json_mode_detail: No content in response (reasoning model may not support JSON mode)

llm_commentary: The latest vLLM run is effectively a tie on throughput with the current leader (5727.9 vs 5728.7 tok/s) but does not beat it, and its TTFT p95 regresses from 26.9 ms to 29.2 ms. That means it misses the latency goal, and because the best balanced point is still the highest-throughput run under the latency cap, it also fails to improve the balanced frontier. The result is still strong and healthy, but the added headroom/features here did not produce a net win over the cleaner vLLM baseline.
docker_command: docker run --name bench-vllm-2240e2c8062f --gpus all --shm-size 16g --network host -v /root/.cache/huggingface:/root/.cache/huggingface -d vllm/vllm-openai:latest --model Qwen/Qwen3.5-9B --host 0.0.0.0 --port 8000 --tensor-parallel-size 1 --dtype auto --gpu-memory-utilization 0.95 --max-model-len 65536 --enable-chunked-prefill --enable-prefix-caching --language-model-only --enable-auto-tool-choice --tool-call-parser qwen3_coder --reasoning-parser qwen3
